unit.name: Features and multimodal fusion
unit.description: Explains how images are featurized and how modalities can be combined through early, middle, and late fusion.
unit.summary: Kiela first notes practical headaches in multimodal learning: one modality can dominate, extra modalities can add noise, coverage may be missing, and model design becomes harder [ts=985s][ts=1008s]. He then reviews image featurization options: region features from object detectors, generic ConvNet features, and Vision Transformer patch embeddings, contrasting them with the more familiar tensorized representation of text [ts=1085s][ts=1149s][ts=1210s]. The core concept is multimodal fusion: combining vectors can be as simple as inner products or linear projections, or more expressive with elementwise multiplication, gating, attention, and bilinear variants [ts=1238s][ts=1285s]. He formalizes the design space as early, middle, and late fusion, and uses FiLM as an example where question representations generate gamma/beta modulation parameters that condition each ResNet block [ts=1302s][ts=1354s].

key_points.text: A major multimodal failure mode is modality dominance, where text can be strong enough that a model learns to ignore the image.
Region features come from running an object detector first and encoding each detected sub-image with a ConvNet.
A Vision Transformer treats image patches as tokens and feeds them through a standard transformer architecture.
Fusion can be early, middle, or late depending on whether modalities interact from the start, after separate processing, or only at the score level.
FiLM conditions a vision network by applying learned multiplicative gamma and additive beta modulation at each ResNet layer.

unit_summary_for_generation: Kiela first notes practical headaches in multimodal learning: one modality can dominate, extra modalities can add noise, coverage may be missing, and model design becomes harder [ts=985s][ts=1008s]. He then reviews image featurization options: region features from object detectors, generic ConvNet features, and Vision Transformer patch embeddings, contrasting them with the more familiar tensorized representation of text [ts=1085s][ts=1149s][ts=1210s]. The core concept is multimodal fusion: combining vectors can be as simple as inner products or linear projections, or more expressive with elementwise multiplication, gating, attention, and bilinear variants [ts=1238s][ts=1285s]. He formalizes the design space as early, middle, and late fusion, and uses FiLM as an example where question representations generate gamma/beta modulation parameters that condition each ResNet block [ts=1302s][ts=1354s].

kp_catalog.name: Multimodal feature extraction
kp_catalog.description: How raw images or other signals are turned into vectors, including region features, ConvNet features, and ViT patch embeddings.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because multimodal feature extraction clarifies how raw images or other signals are turned into vectors, including region features, ConvNet features, and ViT patch embeddings, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Multimodal fusion strategies
kp_catalog.description: The choice of when and how modalities interact, including early, middle, and late fusion, plus modulation and attention-based schemes.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because multimodal fusion strategies formalizes how the choice of when and how modalities interact, including early, middle, and late fusion, plus modulation and attention-based schemes, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
